# Data and Environments: The Reproducibility Gap

By now the daily loop is familiar and your code is safely versioned.
But Chapter 1 made a promise this chapter has to honour: Git versions
*text*, and a reproducible project needs two things that are not text.
It needs the **data** the code consumes, and it needs the **software
environment** the code runs in. Neither belongs in Git as-is, and
neither reproduces on its own. This chapter is about closing that gap:
keeping data and environments out of your history while keeping your
project rebuildable by someone who has neither.

The thesis to carry through: a Git repository full of perfect code is
necessary but not sufficient. If a collaborator clones it and cannot
obtain the data or reconstruct the package versions, your work does not
reproduce, and the gate from Chapter 1 stays shut.

## The two things Git will not capture for you

Git is built for files that are small, text, and meaningfully diffable:
scripts, notes, manuscripts. Data and environments are none of these.

Data is often large, frequently binary, and sometimes restricted
(Chapter 6). Committing a dataset that changes weekly stores every
version forever and bloats the repository permanently, and committing
restricted data can breach a use agreement. The environment, your exact
versions of Python or R and every package, is not a file you write at
all; it is state on your machine that silently drifts as you upgrade.
A result that ran under `polars 1.0` may not even execute under a later
release. Each gap has its own set of tools. Take them in turn.

## Data, strategy one: rebuild it from code {.tier-beginner}

The cleanest solution to the data problem is to not store the data at
all, but to store the *recipe* that produces it. When data is
simulated, or derived deterministically from a public raw source, a
script plus a fixed random seed is smaller, clearer, and more honest
than the data file it generates.

This is exactly how the running example works. Its raw data are built
by a seeded generator, `scripts/00_make_data.py`, and the `.gitignore`
keeps the generated files out of Git entirely. Anyone who clones the
code-only repository rebuilds the inputs with one command:

```bash
$ uv run python scripts/00_make_data.py
wrote data/raw/county_panel.csv  rows=960  counties=40  months=24
$ uv run python scripts/01_clean.py
wrote data/clean/analysis.parquet  rows=948  cols=7
```

The word "seeded" is doing the real work. Because the generator fixes
its random seed, it produces the *same bytes* every time. Run it twice
and the file is identical, which is what makes a downstream result
reproducible rather than merely similar:

```bash
$ md5sum data/raw/county_panel.csv
1b85815d831a97e7f04597a88e89cc50  data/raw/county_panel.csv
$ uv run python scripts/00_make_data.py   # regenerate
wrote data/raw/county_panel.csv  rows=960  counties=40  months=24
$ md5sum data/raw/county_panel.csv        # same hash
1b85815d831a97e7f04597a88e89cc50  data/raw/county_panel.csv
```

Same seed, same hash. And because the data and outputs are ignored,
rebuilding them does not dirty the repository at all:

```bash
$ git status -s

$ git status --ignored -s
!! .venv/
!! data/
!! output/figures/
```

A clean `git status` after a full rebuild is the signal you want: the
code is the version-controlled truth, and the data is a reproducible
by-product of it.

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
"Rebuild from code" is the gold standard, but only if the recipe is
complete and deterministic: a fixed seed, a pinned environment (below),
and a raw source that is either generated or fetched from a stable,
cited location. A script that downloads "the latest" version of a
dataset is not reproducible, because "latest" changes. Pin the data
version the same way you pin everything else.
:::

## Data, strategy two: when you genuinely cannot rebuild it {.tier-advanced}

Real projects often use data that cannot be regenerated from a short
script: a 40 GB administrative extract, licensed vendor data, a
hand-collected survey. You still keep it out of Git, but now you need
somewhere else to put it. The three options below are described from
their documentation and are *not executed in this book's sandbox*
(verified against vendor docs, access date 2026-06-21).

**Git LFS (Large File Storage)** keeps large files in your normal Git
workflow by committing a small *pointer* in place of each file, while
the bytes live on a separate LFS store. It fits files that are large
but still shareable and that you want tracked alongside code. The
catch is cost and quota: GitHub's free tier includes only 1 GB of LFS
storage and 1 GB of bandwidth per month, so a serious dataset will
exceed it quickly. LFS is for a handful of moderately large tracked
files, not a data lake.

**DVC (Data Version Control)** is built for exactly the data-lake case.
Like LFS it commits small metafiles (`.dvc` pointers) to Git, but it
stores the actual data in external remote storage you control (S3,
Google Drive, an SSH server), moved with `dvc push` and `dvc pull`. DVC
versions large data and models without ever putting them in Git, and
is the heavier, more capable tool when data versioning is itself part
of the project.

**A data repository with a DOI** is the right home for the *archived*
version of record. Zenodo accepts up to 50 GB per record, mints a
citable DOI, and can automatically archive a tagged GitHub release.
Dataverse (for example Harvard Dataverse) plays the same role with
institutional backing. This is less about day-to-day versioning and
more about permanent, citable deposit, which is where Chapter 15 picks
it up for the replication package.

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
The decision is not "which tool is best" but "what kind of data is
this." If it rebuilds from code, store the code (strategy one). If it
is a few large but shareable files you actively version, consider LFS
or DVC. If it is the final dataset behind a paper, deposit it in a
repository with a DOI and cite it. What you must never do is the easy
wrong thing: `git add` the raw data into history because it was
convenient today. That is the mistake Chapter 6 exists to prevent, and
it is permanent.
:::

## Capturing the environment: the lockfile {.tier-beginner}

Now the second half of the gap. Even with the right code and the right
data, a result is not reproducible unless the reader can run it under
the same software. The answer is a **lockfile**: a machine-written
record of the exact version of every package (and its dependencies)
your project uses. You do not write it by hand; your package manager
writes it, and you commit it.

This book's example uses `uv`, and its lockfile is `uv.lock`. Until now
that file has lived in the working tree but stayed out of the example's
committed history, so that the earlier chapters' demos saw a clean
four-commit project. This chapter is where the environment formally
enters the record, and it is worth doing carefully, because committing
it changes the project's history.

First, tag the current state so the four-commit baseline the earlier
chapters describe stays referenceable by name:

```bash
$ git tag -a baseline-4commits \
    -m "Four-commit baseline for the Ch.5/6/16 demos (pre-environment)"
$ git tag -n
baseline-4commits Four-commit baseline for the Ch.5/6/16 demos (pre-environment)
```

Then commit the environment files. This is an ordinary commit; the only
thing special about it is what it represents:

```bash
$ git add pyproject.toml uv.lock
$ git commit -m "Pin the software environment with a uv lockfile"
[main c1c408c] Pin the software environment with a uv lockfile
 2 files changed, 2392 insertions(+)
 create mode 100644 pyproject.toml
 create mode 100644 uv.lock
```

The lockfile is large (here, over 2000 lines) precisely because it
pins the *entire* dependency tree, not just the packages you named.
That is the point: it leaves nothing to chance. With it committed, the
project's recipe is complete, and the headline result reproduces from
a clean clone:

```bash
$ uv run python scripts/02_analysis.py
             Estimate  Std. Error  t value  Pr(>|t|)    2.5%   97.5%
Coefficient
treat_post    -0.0189      0.0038  -4.9296       0.0 -0.0267 -0.0112
```

The estimate, a fall of 0.0189 in the teen employment rate, is the
invariant this project is built to protect. It comes back because the
seed fixed the data and the lockfile fixed the environment. Code, data
recipe, and environment: with all three under control, "it works on my
machine" becomes "it works on any machine."

::: {.callout-important .reproducibility title="REPRODUCIBILITY"}
Commit the lockfile (`uv.lock`), not just the loose requirements
(`pyproject.toml`). The requirements say what you want ("polars 1.0 or
newer"); the lockfile says what you actually ran ("polars 1.9.0 and
these exact 59 transitive dependencies"). A reader can satisfy the
first in a dozen incompatible ways. Only the second reproduces your
result.
:::

## The same idea in R and conda {.tier-advanced}

The lockfile pattern is not specific to `uv`; every serious ecosystem
has its version. These are documented from their official guidance and
not executed in this book's sandbox (R and conda are not installed
here; verified 2026-06-21).

In **R**, the `renv` package gives each project a private library and
records exact versions in `renv.lock`. You run `renv::snapshot()` to
write the lockfile and a collaborator runs `renv::restore()` to install
precisely those versions; you commit `renv.lock` (with `.Rprofile` and
`renv/activate.R`) to Git. It is the direct analogue of `uv.lock`.

In the **conda** world there is a sharp and widely missed distinction.
A hand-exported `environment.yml` is *not* a lockfile: `conda env
export` does not guarantee a fully pinned dependency tree and is
platform-specific. To get a true lock you use `conda-lock`, which
solves the environment per platform and pins every package down to the
build string (for example `pandas=2.2.2=py311ha02d727_0`).

::: {.callout-warning .pitfall title="PITFALL"}
Committing an `environment.yml` and believing you are done is one of
the most common false comforts in computational research. It records
your intentions, not your environment. If reproducibility matters, and
for a submitted paper it now does (Chapter 1), commit a real lock:
`uv.lock`, `renv.lock`, or a `conda-lock` file. The loose requirements
file is a convenience for installation, not a guarantee of replication.
:::

## What you can now do

You can state precisely why data and environments fall outside Git,
and choose the right home for each: rebuild data from seeded code when
you can, reach for LFS, DVC, or a DOI-minting repository when you
cannot, and always commit a true lockfile so the environment travels
with the code. Together with version-controlled code, that is a project
that actually reproduces, not one that merely looks tidy. Chapter 15
assembles all of it into a formal replication package and walks it
through a journal's data editor.

## Try it yourself {.unnumbered}

Working in your clone of the running example:

1. Delete the `data/` directory entirely, then run the three scripts in
   order with `uv run`. Confirm the analysis file and the headline
   estimate come back. Explain why deleting the data was safe.
2. Run `git status` after the rebuild. Why is it clean? Name the file
   that makes it clean and the rule inside it that ignores `data/`.
3. Open `uv.lock` and count how many packages it pins versus how many
   are named in `pyproject.toml`. Write one sentence on why the
   difference matters for reproducibility.
4. For a real project of your own, classify its data into the three
   strategies from this chapter (rebuild from code, version with
   LFS/DVC, deposit with a DOI). Which fits, and what would you have to
   change to get there?
